Name the three types of muscle tissue and one key trait of each.
Skeletal: voluntary, striated, multinucleated. Cardiac: involuntary, striated, intercalated discs. Smooth: involuntary, non-striated, spindle-shaped cells.
What is the sliding filament theory?
During contraction, thin actin filaments slide past thick myosin filaments, shortening sarcomeres. Cross-bridges cycle using ATP; Z-discs approach each other.
The functional contractile unit of striated muscle between two Z-discs, containing A band, I band, H zone, and M line.
Describe the role of calcium and troponin-tropomyosin in contraction.
Ca²⁺ binds troponin → tropomyosin shifts off actin binding sites → myosin heads can form cross-bridges. Without Ca²⁺, sites remain blocked (relaxation).
One motor neuron and all the skeletal muscle fibers it innervates. Fine control = small motor units; powerful muscles = large units.
What neurotransmitter is released at the neuromuscular junction of skeletal muscle?
Acetylcholine (ACh). It binds nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate, opening Na⁺ channels and depolarizing the fiber.
Define origin vs insertion of a muscle.
Origin: usually the more proximal, less movable attachment. Insertion: more distal, movable attachment that moves toward the origin during contraction.
What is the primary action of the deltoid muscle?
Abduction of the arm at the shoulder (middle fibers). Anterior fibers flex/medially rotate; posterior extend/laterally rotate.
Which muscle is the primary flexor of the elbow that also supinates the forearm?
Biceps brachii (also a weak shoulder flexor). Brachialis is pure elbow flexor; brachioradialis assists in mid-pronation.
Name the four heads of the quadriceps femoris and their shared action.
Rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius. Shared action: knee extension. Rectus femoris also flexes the hip.
What muscles form the hamstring group and what do they do?
Biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus. They extend the hip and flex the knee (short head of biceps femoris only flexes knee).
What is the primary muscle of inspiration?
The diaphragm. Contraction flattens it, increasing thoracic volume and decreasing intrapulmonary pressure so air flows in.
Which nerve innervates the diaphragm, and which spinal levels form it?
Phrenic nerve from C3–C5 ("C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive").
Isotonic: muscle changes length (concentric shortens, eccentric lengthens under load) while tension is relatively constant. Isometric: tension rises but length stays the same (e.g., holding a pose).
What is the action of sternocleidomastoid when contracting unilaterally vs bilaterally?
Unilateral: rotates head to opposite side and tilts toward same side. Bilateral: flexes the neck.
Name the muscle that is the most powerful hip extensor used when rising from a chair.
What is the difference between a prime mover, antagonist, synergist, and fixator?
Prime mover (agonist): main force for a movement. Antagonist: opposes the movement. Synergist: assists agonist. Fixator: stabilizes origin so agonist acts efficiently.
Where is the Achilles (calcaneal) tendon formed and what is its action?
Formed by gastrocnemius and soleus inserting on the calcaneus. Action: plantarflexion of the ankle (critical for push-off in gait).
Where is Ca²⁺ stored in skeletal muscle fibers and what releases it?
Sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR). An action potential in T-tubules triggers SR Ca²⁺ release via voltage sensors (DHPR) and ryanodine receptors.
What is the action of the pectoralis major?
Adducts and medially rotates the arm; clavicular fibers assist flexion, sternocostal fibers assist extension from flexed position.
What are the two structural divisions of the nervous system?
Central nervous system (brain + spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system (cranial + spinal nerves, ganglia).
Compare the somatic and autonomic nervous systems.
Somatic: voluntary control of skeletal muscle. Autonomic: involuntary control of smooth/cardiac muscle and glands (sympathetic + parasympathetic).
Name the parts of a neuron and the direction of impulse flow.
Dendrites receive → cell body integrates → axon conducts AP → axon terminals release neurotransmitter.
What is myelin and which cells make it in CNS vs PNS?
Insulating sheath that speeds conduction. CNS: oligodendrocytes. PNS: Schwann cells.
Describe resting membrane potential in a typical neuron.
About −70 mV inside relative to outside; maintained mainly by Na+/K+ pump and K+ leak channels.
What is an action potential (threshold, depolarization, repolarization)?
All-or-none spike: threshold opens voltage-gated Na+ channels (depolarization); then Na+ inactivate and K+ exits (repolarization); brief hyperpolarization possible.
Name the four major lobes of the cerebrum and one function each.
Frontal: motor/planning/personality. Parietal: somatosensory. Temporal: hearing/memory. Occipital: vision.
Coordinates voluntary movement, balance, posture, and motor learning (compares intended vs actual movement).
What are the three parts of the brainstem?
Midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata — vital centers (cardio, respiratory), cranial nerve nuclei, ascending/descending tracts.
What is the function of the thalamus and hypothalamus?
Thalamus: sensory relay to cortex (except smell). Hypothalamus: homeostasis (temp, hunger, thirst, autonomic/endocrine control via pituitary).
How many pairs of spinal nerves are there and how are they named?
31 pairs: 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, 1 coccygeal — named for vertebral level (C1–C8, etc.).
Describe a simple stretch reflex arc.
Muscle spindle senses stretch → sensory neuron → spinal cord synapse → motor neuron → same muscle contracts (e.g., patellar reflex).
Sympathetic: fight-or-flight (↑HR, dilate bronchi, mobilize glucose). Parasympathetic: rest-and-digest (↓HR, ↑GI activity). Often dual innervation.
What neurotransmitter is used at most postganglionic sympathetic endings? At parasympathetic endings?
Sympathetic: norepinephrine (except sweat glands use ACh). Parasympathetic: acetylcholine.
How many pairs of cranial nerves are there? Name CN I, II, and X.
12 pairs. CN I olfactory (smell), CN II optic (vision), CN X vagus (major parasympathetic to thorax/abdomen).
What is a synapse and what is a neurotransmitter?
Synapse: junction between neurons (or neuron-effector). Neurotransmitter: chemical messenger released into synaptic cleft (ACh, NE, GABA, glutamate, etc.).
Selective barrier of brain capillary endothelium (tight junctions + astrocytes) that limits many substances from entering brain tissue.
Where does the spinal cord end in adults and what is the cauda equina?
Cord ends near L1–L2 (conus medullaris). Cauda equina is the bundle of lumbar/sacral nerve roots continuing in the canal.
How do hormones travel and affect target cells?
Secreted into blood; act on cells with matching receptors (membrane or intracellular) to change gene expression or cell activity.
Compare steroid vs amino-acid based hormones.
Steroids (lipid-soluble) cross membranes, bind intracellular receptors, alter transcription. Amino-acid based (most) bind membrane receptors → second messengers.
What is the master link between nervous and endocrine systems?
Hypothalamus — controls anterior pituitary via releasing/inhibiting hormones and posterior pituitary via neurohormones.
Name two hormones of the posterior pituitary and their actions.
ADH (vasopressin): water reabsorption in kidney; oxytocin: uterine contraction and milk ejection.
GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, prolactin (and others). Many stimulate other endocrine glands.
Increases basal metabolic rate, heat production, growth/development (especially CNS in infancy). Requires iodine.
What does calcitonin do? What does PTH do?
Calcitonin (thyroid C cells): lowers blood Ca²⁺. PTH (parathyroids): raises blood Ca²⁺ via bone, kidney, vitamin D.
Name the adrenal cortex hormone classes and one example each.
Mineralocorticoids (aldosterone), glucocorticoids (cortisol), androgens. Medulla secretes epinephrine/norepinephrine.
What is the fight-or-flight hormone from the adrenal medulla?
Epinephrine (adrenaline), with some norepinephrine — rapid sympathetic-like effects.
What do insulin and glucagon do to blood glucose?
Insulin (β cells): lowers glucose (uptake, storage). Glucagon (α cells): raises glucose (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis).
Contrast type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus (basic).
Type 1: autoimmune β-cell destruction, absolute insulin lack. Type 2: insulin resistance ± relative insulin deficiency, often lifestyle/obesity linked.
A hormone that stimulates another endocrine gland to secrete its hormone (e.g., TSH → thyroid).
Promotes Na+ reabsorption (and water follows) and K+ secretion in the kidney → raises blood volume/pressure.
What is negative feedback in endocrine control? Give an example.
Rising hormone levels inhibit further release. Example: high T3/T4 inhibits TSH (and TRH).
What is the composition of whole blood by volume roughly?
~55% plasma, ~45% formed elements (mostly RBCs); buffy coat = WBCs + platelets (<1%).
Percentage of blood volume occupied by RBCs (typical adult male ~42–52%, female ~37–47%).
What is the main function of erythrocytes and what protein carries O2?
Transport O2 (and some CO2). Hemoglobin binds O2 in lungs and releases it in tissues.
Where are RBCs produced and what hormone stimulates this?
Red bone marrow; stimulated by erythropoietin (EPO) from kidneys when O2 is low.
Name the five types of leukocytes and a key role for neutrophils and lymphocytes.
Neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils. Neutrophils: acute bacterial infection. Lymphocytes: adaptive immunity (B/T).
Cell fragments from megakaryocytes that form the platelet plug and promote clotting.
Outline the three steps of hemostasis.
1) Vascular spasm 2) Platelet plug formation 3) Coagulation (fibrin clot).
What is the final common pathway product that stabilizes a clot?
Explain ABO blood types and what antibodies each has.
Type A: A antigens, anti-B. Type B: B antigens, anti-A. AB: both antigens, no anti-A/B. O: no A/B antigens, anti-A and anti-B.
Who is the universal donor and universal recipient (ABO only, simplified)?
O− often called universal donor (no A/B antigens); AB+ universal recipient (no anti-A/B, has Rh). Always match carefully clinically.
What is Rh factor and why does it matter in pregnancy?
D antigen on RBCs. Rh− mother exposed to Rh+ fetal blood may make anti-D; later pregnancies risk hemolytic disease of newborn (RhoGAM prevention).
What is anemia? Name one common cause.
Reduced O2-carrying capacity (low RBC count, low Hb, or abnormal Hb). Causes: iron deficiency, blood loss, B12/folate deficiency, hemolysis, chronic disease.
Name the four chambers of the heart and the valves leaving each ventricle.
RA, RV, LA, LV. RV → pulmonary valve → pulmonary trunk. LV → aortic valve → aorta.
Trace a drop of blood through the pulmonary circuit starting at the right atrium.
RA → tricuspid → RV → pulmonary valve → pulmonary trunk/arteries → lungs → pulmonary veins → LA.
Trace systemic circuit from left atrium back to right atrium.
LA → mitral → LV → aortic valve → aorta → body capillaries → venae cavae → RA.
Which side of the heart is thicker and why?
Left ventricle — pumps against higher systemic resistance.
What are the AV valves and semilunar valves?
AV: tricuspid (R), mitral/bicuspid (L). Semilunar: pulmonary and aortic.
Lub (S1): AV valves closing at start of systole. Dub (S2): semilunar valves closing at start of diastole.
What is the SA node and why is it the pacemaker?
Sinoatrial node in RA wall; spontaneously depolarizes fastest → sets heart rate under normal conditions.
List the cardiac conduction pathway in order.
SA node → atrial myocardium/internodal paths → AV node → AV bundle (His) → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers → ventricles.
What is cardiac output and its formula?
CO = heart rate × stroke volume (volume pumped per minute by each ventricle).
Increased preload (EDV/Frank–Starling), increased contractility, decreased afterload (within limits).
Arteries: high pressure, thick walls, away from heart. Capillaries: exchange. Veins: to heart, valves, capacitance vessels.
What is blood pressure and what do systolic/diastolic numbers mean?
Force of blood on vessel walls. Systolic: peak during ventricular contraction. Diastolic: lowest during ventricular relaxation.
Where do the coronary arteries arise and why are they critical?
Arise from aortic sinuses just above the aortic valve; supply myocardium. Occlusion → ischemia/MI.
What is the role of baroreceptors in blood pressure regulation?
Stretch receptors in carotid sinus/aortic arch sense BP; signal CNS to adjust HR, contractility, and vessel tone via ANS.
What is mean arterial pressure (MAP) approximately?
MAP ≈ diastolic + 1/3 (systolic − diastolic); driving pressure for organ perfusion.
What are the main functions of the lymphatic system?
Return interstitial fluid to blood, absorb dietary fats (lacteals), and house immune cells for defense.
What is lymph and how does it form?
Fluid derived from interstitial fluid that enters lymphatic capillaries; similar to plasma but lower protein.
Name primary and secondary lymphoid organs.
Primary: red bone marrow, thymus. Secondary: lymph nodes, spleen, MALT (tonsils, Peyer patches, etc.).
Site of T-lymphocyte maturation; most active in childhood; involutes with age.
What do lymph nodes filter and what cells act there?
Filter lymph; macrophages and lymphocytes (B and T) mount immune responses. Swollen nodes often signal infection.
What are the main functions of the spleen?
Filters blood, removes old RBCs, stores platelets, immune surveillance of blood (white pulp).
Innate: immediate, nonspecific (barriers, phagocytes, inflammation, complement). Adaptive: specific, memory (B/T cells, antibodies).
What is an antigen? What is an antibody?
Antigen: molecule that can trigger adaptive response. Antibody (Ig): protein from B cells that binds antigen specifically.
Differentiate B cells and T cells (basic).
B cells: humoral immunity, become plasma cells → antibodies. T cells: cellular immunity (helper, cytotoxic, regulatory).
What is a vaccine's basic principle?
Expose immune system to antigen safely to generate memory cells without causing full disease.
Local protective response: redness, heat, swelling, pain (± loss of function) — delivers immune cells and promotes healing.
Palatine, pharyngeal (adenoids), and lingual tonsils — part of MALT guarding the pharynx.
Trace the airway path from nose to alveoli.
Nares → nasal cavity → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli.
What structures keep the trachea open?
C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings; trachealis muscle posteriorly.
Where does gas exchange occur and what is the respiratory membrane?
Alveoli. Membrane: alveolar epithelium + capillary endothelium + fused basement membranes (very thin for diffusion).
What is surfactant and why is it important?
Lipoprotein from type II alveolar cells that reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse (atelectasis).
Explain Boyle's law as it relates to breathing.
Pressure of a gas is inversely related to volume. ↑ thoracic volume → ↓ intrapulmonary pressure → air in (inspiration).
What muscles drive quiet inspiration vs forced expiration?
Quiet inspiration: diaphragm + external intercostals. Forced expiration: internal intercostals + abdominal muscles.
Define tidal volume, vital capacity, and residual volume (basic).
TV: air moved in a normal breath. VC: max expired after max inspiration. RV: air left after max expiration (keeps alveoli open).
How is oxygen mostly transported in blood?
Bound to hemoglobin (~98%); small amount dissolved in plasma.
Dissolved, bound to Hb (carbamino), and mostly as bicarbonate (HCO3−) via carbonic anhydrase in RBCs.
What is the primary chemical drive to breathe in healthy people?
Rising PCO2 (and falling pH) via central chemoreceptors; peripheral chemoreceptors also sense low PO2, high PCO2, low pH.
What is the role of the epiglottis?
Covers the laryngeal inlet during swallowing to prevent aspiration into the airway.
External: gas exchange lungs ↔ blood. Internal: gas exchange blood ↔ tissues.
What is compliance of the lung?
How easily lungs expand; reduced in fibrosis, increased in emphysema (with loss of elastic recoil).
Why is the right main bronchus more likely to receive aspirated objects?
It is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left.
List the organs of the alimentary canal in order.
Mouth → pharynx → esophagus → stomach → small intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum) → large intestine → rectum → anus.
Teeth, tongue, salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, pancreas.
What are the four layers of the GI tract wall (inside out)?
Mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, serosa (or adventitia).
What does the stomach secrete that is essential for B12 absorption later?
Intrinsic factor (with HCl and pepsinogen also key secretions).
What is the role of HCl in the stomach?
Activates pepsinogen → pepsin, denatures proteins, kills many microbes, optimal pH for pepsin.
Where does most chemical digestion and absorption occur?
Small intestine (especially duodenum for digestion; jejunum for absorption).
What do bile and pancreatic lipase do for fat digestion?
Bile emulsifies fats (↑ surface area). Pancreatic lipase digests triglycerides to fatty acids + monoglycerides.
Name major pancreatic enzymes and their substrates.
Amylase (starch), lipase (fats), proteases like trypsin/chymotrypsin (proteins), nucleases (nucleic acids).
What is the liver's digestive role?
Produces bile; also processes nutrients, detoxifies, makes plasma proteins (beyond pure digestion).
Stores and concentrates bile; releases it into the duodenum when stimulated (CCK).
What are the main functions of the large intestine?
Absorb water/electrolytes, form/store feces, house microbiota (vitamin K, some B vitamins).
Peristalsis: propulsive waves. Segmentation: mixing contractions in SI that churn chyme with enzymes.
What is the hepatic portal vein's significance?
Carries nutrient-rich blood from GI tract/spleen to the liver for processing before systemic circulation.
Name the three pairs of major salivary glands.
Parotid, submandibular, and sublingual — secrete saliva with amylase, mucus, lysozyme, antibodies.
Name the organs of the urinary system.
What are the main functions of the kidneys?
Filter blood, regulate fluid/electrolytes/acid-base, excrete wastes, produce EPO and renin, activate vitamin D.
What is the functional unit of the kidney?
Glomerulus (capillary tuft) + Bowman's (glomerular) capsule — site of filtration.
List the three processes of urine formation.
Glomerular filtration, tubular reabsorption, tubular secretion.
Glomerular filtration rate — volume of filtrate formed per minute by both kidneys (normal ~125 mL/min).
Proximal convoluted tubule (water, Na+, glucose, amino acids, etc.).
What does ADH do in the collecting duct?
Increases water permeability (aquaporins) → more water reabsorption → concentrated urine; lack of ADH → dilute large volume urine.
What is the juxtaglomerular apparatus and renin?
JGA senses NaCl/pressure; releases renin → RAAS cascade → angiotensin II + aldosterone → raise BP/volume.
The process of emptying the bladder (detrusor contracts, sphincters relax) under autonomic and voluntary control.
Why are females more prone to UTI anatomically?
Shorter urethra and closer proximity to anus/vaginal flora.
What is the difference between the male and female urethra?
Female: short, urine only. Male: longer, carries urine and semen; passes through prostate.
What are the primary male and female gonads and their dual functions?
Testes and ovaries — produce gametes (sperm/ova) and sex hormones (testosterone/estrogen & progesterone).
Where does spermatogenesis occur and what is the path of sperm?
Seminiferous tubules → epididymis (maturation/storage) → ductus (vas) deferens → ejaculatory duct → urethra.
What do seminal vesicles, prostate, and bulbourethral glands contribute?
Seminal vesicles: fructose-rich fluid (most of semen volume). Prostate: enzymes/PSA, milky fluid. Bulbourethral: pre-ejaculate mucus.
What is the role of the scrotum and cremaster?
Keep testes ~3°C cooler than core for spermatogenesis; cremaster elevates testes with temperature/arousal.
Name the layers/path of the female reproductive tract for an oocyte.
Ovary → fimbriae/uterine tube (usual fertilization site) → uterus → cervix → vagina.
What are the three layers of the uterus?
Endometrium (inner lining, sheds in menses), myometrium (smooth muscle), perimetrium (outer serosa).
What hormones dominate the follicular vs luteal phases (basic)?
Follicular: rising estrogen (FSH/LH drive). Luteal: progesterone from corpus luteum (after LH surge/ovulation).
LH surge (mid-cycle) triggered by high estrogen positive feedback.
Where does fertilization usually occur and what is implantation?
Ampulla of uterine tube. Implantation: blastocyst embeds in endometrium (~6–7 days after fertilization).
What does hCG do in early pregnancy?
Maintains corpus luteum so progesterone continues until placenta takes over — basis of pregnancy tests.
